questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is FALSE?
A. There exist context-free languages such that all the context-free grammars generating them are ambiguous.
B. An unambiguous context-free grammar always has a unique parse tree for each string of the language generated by it.
C. Both deterministic and non-deterministic pushdown automata always accept the same set of languages.
D. A finite set of strings over some alphabet is always a regular language.
questions.answer: C. Both deterministic and non-deterministic pushdown automata always accept the same set of languages.
questions.to_find: Which statement about CFLs, CFGs, and pushdown automata is FALSE?

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of key facts about context-free languages, grammars, and pushdown automata. We need to identify which of the four given statements is incorrect. The false statement is about DPDA vs NPDA equivalence, which does NOT hold (unlike DFA vs NFA).

keywords.explain: A CFL for which every possible CFG generating it is ambiguous; no unambiguous grammar exists for it.
keywords.example: L = {a^i b^j c^k | i=j or j=k} is a classic inherently ambiguous CFL.

keywords.explain: A pushdown automaton with at most one move in any configuration; it accepts a strict subset of CFLs called deterministic CFLs (DCFLs).
keywords.example: L = {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} is a CFL not accepted by any DPDA.

keywords.explain: A pushdown automaton that can make multiple transitions from a configuration; it exactly accepts all CFLs.
keywords.example: Every CFL has an NPDA accepting it; DPDA only accepts DCFLs.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='160' viewBox='0 0 520 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='490' height='140' rx='10' class='bg'/><ellipse cx='260' cy='80' rx='220' ry='55' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='260' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>All CFLs (accepted by NPDA)</text><ellipse cx='220' cy='90' rx='130' ry='35' fill='none' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='220' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>DCFLs (accepted by DPDA)</text><text x='420' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>NPDA only</text><text x='420' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(e.g. ww^R)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing DCFLs as a strict subset of all CFLs. NPDAs accept all CFLs; DPDAs accept only DCFLs.

given.aim: Identify the false statement among four claims about CFLs, CFGs, and pushdown automata.

terms.meaning: A CFL for which every CFG is ambiguous
terms.example: L = {a^i b^j c^k | i=j or j=k}

terms.meaning: A CFG where every string has exactly one parse tree
terms.example: S -> aSb | epsilon is unambiguous for {a^n b^n}
terms.connects: Makes statement B TRUE by definition

terms.meaning: DPDA accepts only DCFLs; NPDA accepts all CFLs; unlike DFA/NFA these are NOT equivalent
terms.example: ww^R requires NPDA; no DPDA accepts it

terms.meaning: A language with finitely many strings is always regular
terms.example: {ab, ba, aab} is regular (can build a finite DFA)

given.plan: Check each statement against known TOC facts. DFA and NFA are equivalent, but DPDA and NPDA are NOT equivalent — DPDA accepts a strict subset of what NPDA accepts. Statement C falsely claims they accept the same set.

steps.title: Evaluate statement A
steps.apply: Inherently ambiguous CFLs do exist (e.g., {a^i b^j c^k | i=j or j=k}). For such languages, every possible CFG is ambiguous. So statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: Inherently ambiguous languages are a known class within CFLs.

steps.title: Evaluate statement B
steps.apply: By definition, an unambiguous CFG produces exactly one parse tree (leftmost or rightmost derivation) for each string in the language. Statement B is TRUE.
steps.note: This is literally the definition of unambiguity.

steps.title: Evaluate statement C
steps.apply: Unlike the DFA/NFA case, DPDA and NPDA are NOT equivalent. DPDA accepts only deterministic CFLs (DCFLs), which is a strict subset of all CFLs. For example, L = {ww^R | w in {a,b}*} is a CFL accepted by an NPDA but no DPDA. Statement C is FALSE.
steps.note: This is a crucial distinction: DFA = NFA in power, but DPDA != NPDA in power.

steps.title: Evaluate statement D
steps.apply: Any finite language over any alphabet is regular. We can always build a finite automaton (trie-like DFA) to accept a finite set of strings. Statement D is TRUE.
steps.note: Every finite language is regular by the closure property and constructive argument.

solution.result: Statement C is FALSE. Deterministic and non-deterministic pushdown automata do NOT accept the same set of languages; NPDA is strictly more powerful than DPDA.

questions.formula_note: Key distinction: DFA and NFA are equivalent in power, but DPDA and NPDA are NOT equivalent. DCFLs (accepted by DPDA) are a strict subset of CFLs (accepted by NPDA).

questions.question:
Let Sigma = {1, 2, 3, 4}. For x in Sigma*, let prod(x) be the product of symbols in x modulo 7. We take prod(epsilon) = 1, where epsilon is the null string. For example, prod(124) = (1 * 2 * 4) mod 7 = 1.
Define L = {x in Sigma* : prod(x) mod 7 = 0}.
The number of states in a minimum state DFA for L is __________ . (Answer in integer)
questions.to_find: Number of states in the minimum DFA for L = {x in {1,2,3,4}* : prod(x) mod 7 = 0}

understand.plain: We consider strings over {1,2,3,4} and track the product of all symbols modulo 7. We want strings where this product is 0 mod 7 (i.e., divisible by 7). Since no symbol in {1,2,3,4} is divisible by 7, the product of any such string can never be 0 mod 7, making L an empty language.

keywords.explain: The product of all symbols in a string, taken modulo 7. Since 1,2,3,4 are all coprime to 7, their product is always coprime to 7.
The minimum DFA for the empty language (no string accepted) has exactly 1 state — a single non-accepting (dead/trap) state.
keywords.example: A single state q0 (non-accepting) with all transitions looping back to q0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='160' viewBox='0 0 400 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='390' height='150' rx='8' class='bg'/><circle cx='200' cy='85' r='40' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><text x='200' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>q0</text><text x='200' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(non-accept)</text><path d='M 200 45 C 240 10 280 10 280 45 C 280 65 250 70 230 62' fill='none' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='270' y='28' class='sub mut'>1,2,3,4</text><text x='200' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Min DFA: 1 state, 0 accept states</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='#5F5E5A'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Minimum DFA for empty language: single non-accepting state q0 with all transitions (on symbols 1,2,3,4) looping back to q0.

given.aim: Find the number of states in the minimum DFA for L = {x in {1,2,3,4}* : product of symbols mod 7 = 0}.

terms.meaning: The input alphabet; each symbol is one of 1,2,3,4
terms.connects: All symbols are in range 1-6 mod 7, so coprime to 7

terms.meaning: Product of all symbols in x, reduced modulo 7
terms.connects: We want prod(x) mod 7 = 0, i.e., product divisible by 7

terms.meaning: A number whose GCD with 7 is 1; multiplying coprimes of 7 always gives a coprime of 7
terms.connects: Since all symbols are coprime to 7, no product can ever be divisible by 7

given.plan: Determine the set L by checking whether prod(x) mod 7 = 0 is achievable. Since {1,2,3,4} are all coprime to 7, their product is always coprime to 7, meaning L is empty. The minimum DFA for an empty language has exactly 1 state.

steps.title: Check if any string in {1,2,3,4}* can have product divisible by 7
steps.apply: The symbols are from {1,2,3,4}. Check GCD with 7: gcd(1,7)=1, gcd(2,7)=1, gcd(3,7)=1, gcd(4,7)=1. All symbols are coprime to 7. The product of coprime-to-7 numbers is also coprime to 7 (since 7 is prime). Therefore prod(x) mod 7 is in {1,2,3,4,5,6} for any non-empty x, and prod(epsilon) = 1. The value 0 mod 7 is never achievable.
steps.note: Key insight: 7 is prime, and none of 1,2,3,4 is a multiple of 7, so their product never introduces a factor of 7.

steps.title: Determine the language L and its minimum DFA
steps.apply: Since prod(x) mod 7 = 0 is impossible for any x in {1,2,3,4}*, the language L = empty set. The minimum DFA for the empty language consists of exactly 1 state: a single non-accepting (dead/trap) state q0. All transitions on input symbols 1,2,3,4 loop back to q0. No state is an accepting state.
steps.note: The minimum DFA for the empty language always has exactly 1 state. A 0-state DFA is not valid since we need at least one state (the start state).

solution.result: The number of states in the minimum DFA for L is 1.

questions.formula_note: Key insight: since 7 is prime and all symbols in {1,2,3,4} are coprime to 7, no product of symbols can be 0 mod 7, making L the empty language. Minimum DFA for empty language has exactly 1 state.

questions.question:
For Sigma = {a, b}, let us consider the regular language L = {x | x = a^(2n-18) or x = b^(3n+18), for some n >= 0, with 2n-18 >= 0}.
Which one of the following can be a pumping length (the constant guaranteed by the pumping lemma) for L?
A. 3
B. 5
C. 9
D. 24
questions.to_find: A valid pumping length for the given regular language L

understand.plain: The pumping lemma for regular languages says that for any regular language L, there exists a pumping length p such that any string in L of length >= p can be 'pumped' (a middle portion repeated). We need to find which of the given values (3,5,9,24) can serve as such a pumping length for the language L consisting of even-power strings of a and long strings of b.

keywords.explain: The constant p in the pumping lemma; any string in L with length >= p can be split as xyz where |xy| <= p, |y| >= 1, and xy^i z is in L for all i >= 0.
keywords.example: For L = a*, p=1 works: any string a^n = a^1 * a^(n-1) can be pumped.

keywords.explain: A value p is a VALID pumping length for L if every string in L of length >= p satisfies the pumping condition. Any p >= (min DFA states) is valid; smaller values may also work depending on the language.
keywords.example: If a DFA has 5 states, p=5 is valid, and so is p=6,7,... but p=3 might not work.

understand.visual_alt: L consists of two parts: strings a^(2n-18) for n >= 9 (i.e., a^0, a^2, a^4,...) and strings b^(3n+18) for n >= 0 (i.e., b^18, b^21, b^24,...). The DFA for L must handle both patterns.

given.aim: Find a value among {3, 5, 9, 24} that can serve as a valid pumping length for L.

terms.meaning: Strings a^k where k is even and k >= 0; i.e., a^0=epsilon, a^2, a^4, a^6, ...
terms.example: n=9 gives a^0, n=10 gives a^2, n=11 gives a^4
terms.connects: These are strings of even length over {a}, including epsilon

terms.meaning: Strings b^k where k is in {18, 21, 24, 27, ...} (multiples of 3 starting from 18)
terms.example: n=0 gives b^18, n=1 gives b^21
terms.connects: These are strings of length >= 18 over {b}, with length = 0 mod 3 shifted by 18

terms.meaning: The threshold above which every string in L can be pumped; p is at least the number of states in the minimum DFA
terms.example: If min DFA has 5 states, any p >= 5 is valid
terms.connects: We check whether each candidate value satisfies all conditions of the pumping lemma for L

given.plan: Analyze the structure of L. Identify the minimum number of states needed in a DFA for L to determine the minimum valid pumping length. Then check each candidate against the pumping lemma conditions.

steps.title: Identify the structure of L
steps.formula_raw: L = {a^k : k even, k >= 0} union {b^k : k >= 18, k = 0 mod 3 after shift}
steps.apply:
Part 1: 2n - 18 >= 0 means n >= 9. The values of 2n-18 for n = 9, 10, 11,... are 0, 2, 4, 6,... So part 1 is {epsilon, a^2, a^4, a^6, ...} = all even-length strings of a's (including empty).
Part 2: 3n+18 for n >= 0 gives 18, 21, 24, 27,... So part 2 is {b^18, b^21, b^24,...}.
steps.note: Part 1 is all even powers of a; Part 2 is powers of b that are multiples of 3 starting at 18.

steps.title: Check pumping length p = 3
steps.apply: Consider the string a^4 in L (|a^4| = 4 >= 3). We must split as xyz with |xy| <= 3, |y| >= 1. So y = a^k for 1 <= k <= 3. Then xy^0 z = xz = a^(4-k). For this to be in L, 4-k must be even, so k must be even. But k can be 1 (odd), in which case a^3 would need to be in L, but a^3 is not (since part 1 requires even exponent). So p=3 fails.
steps.note: p=3 does not work because a^4 with y=a cannot be pumped down.

steps.title: Check pumping length p = 5
steps.apply:
For p=5, consider any string s in L with |s| >= 5. Case 1: s = a^(2m) for m >= 3 (|s| >= 6 >= 5). Split as x=epsilon, y=a^2, z=a^(2m-2). Then |xy|=2<=5, |y|=2>=1, and xy^i z = a^(2i + 2m-2) = a^(2(i+m-1)) which is even for all i >= 0 -- in L. Case 2: s = b^(3n+18) with |s| >= 5 means b^18 or longer. Split y = b^3. Then xy^i z = b^(3n+18 - 3 + 3i) = b^(3(n+i-1)+18) -- in L for i >= 1. For i=0: b^(3n+15) which is not in L since 3n+15 < 18 requires n < 1, and for n >= 1 we get b^(3n+15) which equals b^(3(n-1)+18) -- this IS in L for n >= 1! For n=0: b^15, which needs to be checked: 15 = 3n+18 means n=-1, not valid. Hmm, b^15 not in L. But b^18 (n=0) with y=b^3: pumping down gives b^15, not in L. So y=b^3 doesn't work directly for b^18. Instead use y=b^3 with |xy| <= 5, so we can choose y=b^3 starting at any position. b^18 split as x=b^0, y=b^3, z=b^15 -- i=0 gives b^15 not in L.

Actually for p=5, the valid pumping approach for b^18 (|b^18|=18 >= 5): choose y = b^3, but we need xy^i z in L for all i. xy^0 z = b^15 is not in L. This seems to fail.

However, the pumping lemma says there EXISTS a decomposition xyz -- not that EVERY decomposition works. For b^18: x = b^0, y = b^3, z = b^15 -- pumping UP: b^18+3i in L for i >= 0 since 18+3i = 3(i+0)+18, yes! But i=0 gives b^15... wait i=0: xy^0 z = xz = b^0 b^15 = b^15, not in L. So this decomposition fails for i=0.

The pumping lemma requires xy^i z in L for ALL i >= 0. So b^18 with ANY split where |xy|<=5, |y|>=1 must allow pumping. All such y have y = b^k, 1 <= k <= 5. xy^0 z = b^(18-k). 18-k in {13,14,15,16,17} -- none of these are in L (L requires b^18 or more with step 3). So p=5 fails for b^18!

Let me reconsider. p=24 might be needed since shortest b-string is b^18 and we can only pump b-strings of length >= 18. But for b^18: if p=24, then b^18 has length 18 < 24, so the pumping condition doesn't apply to it. We only need to pump strings of length >= p. For p=24, strings of length < 24 in L need not be pumpable.

So p=24: shortest b-string that needs pumping is b^24 (=b^(3*2+18)). Split b^24 as x=epsilon, y=b^3, z=b^21. xy^i z = b^(3i+21) = b^(3(i+1)+18) in L for i >= 0 (giving b^21, b^24,...). For i=0: b^21 = b^(3*1+18) in L. Good! For a-strings: a^(2m) with 2m >= 24, m >= 12. Split y=a^2, rest is even -- works. So p=24 works.
steps.note: After careful analysis, p=24 is the correct valid pumping length. p=5 does not satisfy the pumping lemma for strings like b^18 through b^21. p=24 ensures all strings requiring pumping have the right structure.

steps.title: Verify p = 24
steps.apply:
With p=24: strings in L of length >= 24 are: a^(2m) for 2m >= 24 (m >= 12), and b^(3n+18) for 3n+18 >= 24 (n >= 2), i.e., b^24, b^27,...
For a^(2m), m >= 12: split x=epsilon, y=a^2, z=a^(2m-2). |xy|=2<=24, |y|=2. xy^i z = a^(2+2i+2m-2) = a^(2m+2i-2)... let me use y=a^2 at the start. Actually more simply: for a^(2m) pick y = a^2 at any position; xy^i z = a^(2m-2+2i) = a^(2(m-1+i)) is even and >= 0 for i >= 0. In L. Good.
For b^(3n+18), n >= 2: split x=epsilon, y=b^3, z=b^(3n+15). xy^i z = b^(3i + 3n+15) = b^(3(i+n-1)+18). For i=0: b^(3(n-1)+18) with n-1 >= 1, so this is in L. For all i >= 0: 3(i+n-1)+18 with i+n-1 >= n-1 >= 1 >= 0. In L. Good.
So p=24 is valid.
steps.note: p=24 works because all strings in L of length >= 24 can be pumped while remaining in L.

solution.result: D. 24 is a valid pumping length for L. The shortest b-string (b^18) has length 18 < 24, so it need not be pumpable. All strings in L of length >= 24 can be pumped while staying in L.

questions.formula_note: Pumping lemma for regular languages: for any regular language L there exists p such that every string s in L with |s| >= p can be written as xyz with |xy| <= p, |y| >= 1, and xy^i z in L for all i >= 0. The minimum valid p is the number of states in the minimum DFA.

questions.question:
A language L satisfies the Pumping Lemma for regular languages, and also the Pumping Lemma for context-free languages. Which of the following statements about L is TRUE?
A. L is necessarily a regular language.
B. L is necessarily a context-free language, but not necessarily a regular language.
C. L is necessarily a context-sensitive language, but not necessarily a context-free language.
D. None of the above.
questions.to_find: What can we conclude about language L if it satisfies both the pumping lemma for regular languages and for CFLs?

understand.plain: The pumping lemma provides a necessary condition for a language to be regular or context-free. Satisfying the pumping lemma does NOT guarantee that the language is regular or context-free. A language that satisfies both pumping lemmas may still be non-regular, non-context-free, or even recursively enumerable. So none of statements A, B, C are guaranteed.

keywords.explain: The pumping lemma is a necessary but NOT sufficient condition. If L is regular, it satisfies the pumping lemma. But satisfying the pumping lemma does NOT imply L is regular.
keywords.example: L = {a^n b^n c^n} is not CFL, yet it might satisfy the CFL pumping lemma for specific pumping lengths (it doesn't, but the point is satisfying a pumping lemma doesn't prove membership in a class).

keywords.explain: The converse is false: a non-regular language can satisfy the regular pumping lemma, and a non-CFL can satisfy the CFL pumping lemma.
keywords.example: {a^p : p is prime} satisfies some forms of pumping conditions yet is non-regular.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' viewBox='0 0 480 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='470' height='190' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='20' y='20' width='200' height='80' rx='6' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='120' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L is regular</text><text x='120' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>=> L satisfies</text><text x='120' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>pumping lemma</text><text x='255' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='big mut'>-></text><text x='340' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl no'>L satisfies</text><text x='340' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl no'>pumping lemma</text><text x='340' y='92' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>does NOT imply</text><text x='340' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>L is regular</text><text x='240' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Pumping lemma: NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT</text><text x='240' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Satisfying both pumping lemmas => no guarantee about L's class</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Arrow diagram showing: Regular L implies pumping lemma holds, but the converse is false. Satisfying the pumping lemma does not imply the language is regular or CFL.

given.aim: Determine what membership in a language class can be concluded when a language satisfies BOTH the regular and CFL pumping lemmas.

terms.meaning: A property that ALL regular languages satisfy; if L is regular, for large enough strings, a substring can be repeated
terms.example: For L=a*, any string a^n (n>=1) can be pumped: a^n = a*a*a^(n-1), pump middle part
terms.connects: Satisfying this lemma is necessary for regularity, not sufficient

terms.meaning: A property that ALL CFLs satisfy; longer strings can have two substrings pumped simultaneously
terms.example: For L={a^n b^n}, the string a^n b^n can be pumped with u=a^i, v=a^j, ...
terms.connects: Satisfying this lemma is necessary for CFL membership, not sufficient

terms.meaning: Pumping lemma is necessary: if L is regular, it satisfies the lemma. It is not sufficient: satisfying the lemma does not guarantee L is regular
terms.example: There exist non-regular, non-CFL languages that still satisfy the pumping lemma
terms.connects: No positive conclusion about L's language class can be drawn from satisfying both pumping lemmas

given.plan: Recall that pumping lemmas provide only necessary conditions. Satisfying a pumping lemma does not prove a language belongs to the corresponding class. Therefore neither regular, CFL, nor context-sensitive membership can be guaranteed.

steps.title: Recall the role of pumping lemmas
steps.formula_raw: L regular => L satisfies regular pumping lemma (but NOT converse)
steps.apply: The pumping lemma for regular languages is a necessary condition for regularity. It is used to prove languages are NOT regular (by showing the property fails). However, if a language satisfies the pumping lemma, we cannot conclude it is regular. Similarly for CFLs.
steps.note: The pumping lemma is a one-way implication: class membership implies lemma satisfaction, NOT vice versa.

steps.title: Check each option
steps.apply:
A: 'L is necessarily regular' -- FALSE. There exist non-regular languages that satisfy the regular pumping lemma (e.g., {a^i b^j c^k | i < j < k} might satisfy it for some pumping length).
B: 'L is necessarily CFL but not regular' -- FALSE. Same argument; satisfying CFL pumping lemma does not make L a CFL.
C: 'L is necessarily context-sensitive but not CFL' -- FALSE and too strong a claim; no such guarantee.
D: 'None of the above' -- This is correct. We cannot determine L's language class just from it satisfying both pumping lemmas.
steps.note: There exist languages outside the Chomsky hierarchy that satisfy both pumping lemmas; the lemmas are not characterizations, only necessary conditions.

solution.result: D. None of the above. Satisfying both pumping lemmas does not guarantee L belongs to any specific language class.

questions.formula_note: Pumping lemmas are necessary but not sufficient conditions for language class membership. A language satisfying the pumping lemma for regular languages is not necessarily regular, and similarly for CFLs.
